sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge, Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Complaint & incident escalation**: no documented path for member complaints, injuries, safeguarding concerns, or near-misses; critical for duty of care and insurance.
- **New member onboarding flow**: no step-by-step SOP covering first contact → payment setup → induction → coach assignment → first class experience.
- **Retention & win-back**: no process for identifying churn risk, re-engagement campaigns, or exit interviews; marketing SOPs likely cover acquisition only.
- **Financial close & reconciliation**: no monthly close procedure, payment reconciliation, refund authority, or expense approval documented.
- **Health & safety incident reporting**: no formal log, investigation, or corrective action SOP (separate from complaints).
- **Class cancellation & rescheduling**: no protocol for coach absence cover, member notification, or credit/reschedule policy.
- **Member termination & offboarding**: no exit process (final payment, data deletion, contract closure).
- **Coaching performance management**: no process for observation, feedback, underperformance handling, or retraining.

**Needs updating**

- **Coach hiring & onboarding (Coaches 17/17)**: likely missing background check procedure, safeguarding training sign-off, probation review, and induction checklist.
- **Marketing (10/12)**: two gaps (newsletter, blog) suggest retention comms and content calendar are missing or need formalization.
- **Facilities (4/4)**: probably gaps in emergency procedures (fire, injury, evacuation), equipment maintenance logs, and cleaning protocols.
- **Client communication**: likely no standard response time SOP for inquiries, complaint acknowledgment, or member communication cadence.
- **Pricing & promotion**: no documented approval workflow for discount codes, class pricing changes,

kpi.advice:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## REVENUE — PRIORITY AREA

**Active clients grew 36% (108→147 over Apr–Jun), but revenue is flat/declining (£18.6k→£17.7k).** This is your biggest red flag. The new studio should be driving revenue *up*, not down.

• **Raise membership pricing immediately.** AR/M (average revenue per member) collapsed from £171.81 (April) to £120.48 (June) — a 30% fall. You're undercharging for the expanded capacity. Implement a tiered pricing model (basic/premium) and shift new joiners to higher tier; existing members stay on current rate. Target £160+ AR/M.

• **Plug class capacity leakage.** You have 147 members in a space 2× the old size but revenue is lower than when you had 108. Classes are running under-full. Introduce peak-hour surge pricing (7–9am, 5–7pm premiums of +£2–3/class), shift off-peak (11am–3pm) to discounted rates or longer-commitment packages to fill the space.

• **Launch high-margin upsells.** Add personal training (even 1:1 intro sessions at £25 gross margin), nutrition plans (£9.99/month SaaS or paper guides), branded merchandise, or corporate wellness contracts (partner with local employers for bulk memberships). Non-dues revenue should add 8–12% to gross profit.

• **Reverse churn spike.** June churn was 0% but May was 5.74% — April's move caused attrition. Implement a 90-day post-relaunch win-back campaign (email/SMS 15 lapsed members with 2-week free trial; likely recovery rate 30%, adding ~£800 gross revenue for minimal cost).

---

## EXPENSES — SECONDARY PRIORITY

**Non-people costs spiked Apr–Jun (£30.5k→£9.4k→£9.4k).** April was one-time fit-out; Jan–Mar spikes (£8.2k–£15.6k) suggest recurring vendor overbilling or untracked invoices.

• **Audit non-people expenses month-by-month.** June sits at £9.39k (reasonably aligned to pre-move baseline of £4.3–£5.7k), but Jan–Mar averaged £11.4k — 2× normal. Pull supplier invoices for rent, utilities, insurance, equipment leases, cleaning, software. Flag any recurring charges

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her 17 Build: Lower, 9 Build: Upper, 6 Elevate and 3 Strongevity sessions (35 classes) must shift to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo is already carrying Build and Elevate; Eilis can take Strongevity and some Elevate to build confidence before Tuesdays become possible later.

• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions**: She's running 44 PT slots *plus* 15 Build: Upper and 20 scans. Offload 6–8 of her PT clients to Eilis (who has only 11) to free JoJo's diary for mentoring and strategic work, and build Eilis's PT confidence and billable hours.

• **Move Body Composition scans off both coaches' timetables**: All 9 of Grace's scans and ~10–15 of JoJo's can be handled by admin or a junior, freeing real coaching capacity. This alone recovers ~25 hours of mislabelled time.

• **Close the Circuits and Mobility gap**: JoJo is the only coach offering Circuits (6 sessions) and Mobility (7 sessions). Add one Mobility or Circuits slot per week to Eilis's rota to build variety, reduce JoJo dependency, and keep member choice live.

• **Right-size the timetable for realistic PT demand**: If Grace's 9 PT hours and some of JoJo's 44 genuinely move to Eilis, clarify which PT slots are real revenue vs. complimentary intro or assessment. Cap Eilis at 15–18 PT slots and JoJo at 25–30 to prevent burnout and create cover redundancy.

•

class_stats.suggestion:
**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. Launch a paid "Open Gym + Coach" block on Monday and Thursday evenings (your quietest days) — 30-45 min slots where members can train independently with a coach available for form checks or quick programming advice. Charge £5-8 per session to offset staffing and generate revenue during dead time.

2. Run a weekly "Barbell Basics" workshop on Saturday mornings (quietest day, lowest attendance overall) — 20-30 min technique drop-in before or after a class, targeting members intimidated by Build classes and those who've attended Barbelle (only 12 in 30 days). Make it free or £3 to build pipeline into paid classes.

**Lift the quieter classes:**

3. Move "Engine" and "Capacity" to Wednesday or Tuesday mornings/lunch slots (your peak days) and pair with a two-week targeted email campaign to members who've attended Build classes, framing them as complementary conditioning work. These conditioning classes likely appeal to the same strength-focused audience but are buried in weak time slots.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Audit what you're actually selling RIGHT NOW**
Walk the gym floor. Interview 3-5 of your best coaches and 5-10 loyal members separately. Ask: "What do people think they're paying for?" You'll likely find misalignment. Document everything they say—this becomes your menu's foundation, not what you *think* you offer.

**2. Build two separate menus, physically different**
- **Coach menu**: A single-page laminated sheet listing packages, add-ons (PT sessions, nutrition coaching, form checks), pricing, and exactly what hands-on support each level gets. Make it their sales tool.
- **Member menu**: A simple poster + digital version showing tiers clearly (basic membership vs. premium), what's included in each, cost per month/annual, and one-sentence descriptions members actually understand.

**3. Price backwards from your target revenue, not competitor rates**
Figure out your monthly revenue goal. Divide by your current member count. That's your minimum average spend per member. Now design packages that hit that number when 60% of members upgrade to mid-tier. Don't guess—do the math first.

**4. Test the menu with 5 members before launch**
Show them drafts. Ask: "Would you upgrade to this?" Listen for friction. If they hesitate, the menu's unclear or priced wrong. Adjust once based on their feedback, then lock it in.

**5. Brief your coaches with role-play first**
Spend 20 minutes with each coach selling you a package using the new menu. Watch where they struggle. That's where the menu confuses clients too. Fix the language before it hits the gym

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement Action Plan for Grace

**1. Audit your actual drop-off points first (not guesses)**
Pull your analytics right now—where are visitors leaving? Homepage? Pricing? Class details? Focus your refinement there, not spreading thin across all pages. If 60% bounce on "What We Offer," that's your priority.

**2. Steal from your existing customer conversations**
Before redesigning anything, compile the 10-15 most common questions you actually hear from prospects (WhatsApp, inquiries, trials). Turn those exact phrases into your FAQ section and page headers. Grace, your real prospects' language > any copywriter's assumptions.

**3. Make "women's-only" positioning crystal clear in the hero section**
If this is a differentiator (and it should be), don't bury it. Test a headline like "Strength Training Without Judgment—For Women Only" above the fold. Measure if clarity here reduces tire-kickers and attracts your ideal member faster.

**4. Add a specific "What's Included" visual breakdown**
Stop describing your offering in paragraphs. Create a simple 4-5 box visual: Classes You'll Access | Community Features | Coaching Included | Equipment/Space. Make it scannable in 5 seconds—people don't read, they skim.

**5. A/B test a single high-impact change this week**
Pick ONE page element (headline, CTA button text, or FAQ addition). Change it, run it for 7 days, measure conversions/inquiries. Learn what actually moves *your* Belfast audience, then scale that insight.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Implement Member Feedback System — Execution Plan for The Forge

• **Start with post-class verbal check-ins, not forms.** Grab 2-3 members after your busiest class each week. Ask: "What's one thing we nailed?" and "One thing to improve?" Takes 90 seconds per person. You'll spot genuine friction points (temperature, music, instructor pace) that a form never captures. Use these insights to decide *if* a formal system is even needed.

• **If you do a form, embed it into existing touchpoints.** Don't ask members to "fill out a survey." Instead: QR code on the water station linked to a 4-question Google Form they complete while cooling down. Or: one question asked by reception staff at sign-out ("How was class today?") with answers logged in a spreadsheet. Friction kills compliance on both ends.

• **Make feedback loops visible and fast.** When someone suggests better music playlists, implement it within 2 weeks and tell *that member* you did it. Post monthly wins on your gym notice board: "You asked for a 6am spin class—launching next month." Members who see action repeat feedback; those ignored stop giving it.

• **Assign one person as feedback owner.** That's you or a trusted manager. One person reviews weekly, spots patterns, flags issues. Prevents feedback from disappearing into a spreadsheet graveyard. Schedule 30 mins every Friday to action it.

• **Target the revenue question first.** Before launching, identify: Will this feedback help you upsell services, reduce churn, or justify premium pricing? Focus early questions on *that* outcome. Feedback for feedback's sake is admin noise.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Audit first, then document**
Don't write from scratch—spend 2 hours this week shadowing each service (or interviewing whoever runs it). You'll spot inconsistencies that matter: Does every PT session start the same way? Does Fuel Forward have a standard onboarding? Document what's *actually* happening, then optimize. This prevents SOPs that look good but don't match reality.

**2. Use a dead-simple template & assign owners**
Create ONE template (max 1 page per SOP): Purpose → Pre-service checklist → During → Post (follow-up/payment/feedback). Assign each service owner to draft their own SOP—they know the gaps better than you. You review & standardize language. This takes weeks, not months, and they'll actually use it.

**3. Build SOPs around revenue protection & retention**
For each, embed the commercial bits: membership SOPs should include "when/how to upsell PT," PT should include "recovery product pitch timing," Fuel Forward should have a "reorder touchpoint." Well75 and Strongevity need clear progression paths that naturally upsell members. Operational efficiency matters less than fixing leaky revenue steps.

**4. Test one SOP live immediately**
Pick the easiest (probably membership onboarding) and run it for 2 weeks with your team. Collect feedback. Refine. Then roll remaining SOPs—don't wait for perfect. You'll learn what actually sticks faster than planning everything.

**5. Link SOPs to weekly metrics**
Each SOP needs one metric: PT adherence rate, Fuel Forward repeat orders, Well75 class attendance

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with one platform only** — Pick Instagram (highest ROI for fitness). Nail 3-4/week there before expanding to TikTok or Facebook. Use a free template (Notion or Canva's scheduling feature) — don't buy software yet. Set it up this week for the next 4 weeks only.

• **Build content in batches, not daily** — Block 2 hours every Sunday. Film 8-12 short clips of members mid-workout, transformations, class highlights using your phone. Batch-edit into 4 posts. This kills the "I don't have time" problem that kills most calendars.

• **Create a 4-post weekly formula that actually sells** — Post 1: Member win/transformation (builds social proof), Post 2: Class/trainer spotlight (community feel), Post 3: Fitness tip or myth-bust (value), Post 4: Behind-the-scenes or event promo (FOMO). Rotate this. Predictability = engagement habit.

• **Assign ownership and automate scheduling** — Get one team member (or hire 5 hours/week freelancer for £25) to schedule posts on Wednesday for the week ahead using Buffer or Later. Grace reviews Sunday batch content. Non-negotiable: calendar must be locked 7 days ahead.

• **Measure what matters: clicks to trial bookings** — Add a unique link or promo code to every post. Track which post types drive signups. After 4 weeks, double down on the 2 formats that convert. Abandon the rest.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# How Grace Should Execute: Regular Videography with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW with a standing booking** — Don't email Jordan "let's schedule." Call today, book 8-week cycles for the next 12 months upfront (so Q1-Q4 slots are gone). This removes the friction that kills consistency. Build it into your gym's operational calendar like payroll.

• **Pre-plan content pillars for each session** — Before Jordan arrives, nail down what 3-4 video assets you need (e.g., member transformation story, class highlight reel, equipment demo). Send him the list 1 week prior. This stops vague "film stuff" sessions and ensures every 2 hours of filming produces 6-8 usable clips.

• **Dedicate one quiet gym hour** — Schedule Jordan during off-peak time (e.g., 2-3pm weekday) when you can isolate 2-3 members for testimonials or showcase equipment without chaos. A packed gym = unusable audio and scattered footage.

• **Repurpose ruthlessly across 8 weeks** — Don't dump all content at once. Release one short clip weekly to Instagram/TikTok + one email to your list between sessions. This keeps you visible without needing constant new filming.

• **Set one hard KPI per session** — E.g., "grow Instagram followers 50" or "generate 2 qualified leads from video testimonials." Track what converts; tell Jordan what to adjust next cycle.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# 6-Week Trial Automation Overhaul for The Forge

• **Audit the leak first**: Pull your last 30 trial enquiries. Track where each dropped (never booked? booked but no-show? attended once then quit?). This tells you which automation step is actually broken — probably it's weak follow-up sequencing, not the system itself.

• **Rebuild the sequence as: Day 1 (welcome + class schedule PDF), Day 3 (social proof video or member testimonial), Day 5 (remove friction—book their first class FOR them with a specific time/trainer), Day 10 (check-in: "How did it go?"). This is specifically for women's fitness psychology—they need belonging signals, not just gym info.

• **Set it to trigger off the form submission**, not manual entry. Connect Typeform → Zapier → your email/SMS provider. Spend 30 mins getting this live this week rather than waiting for "perfect." Imperfect automation beats manual follow-up every time.

• **Add a 48-hour human touchpoint**: The best automation is boring—a 6am text from *you* or a team member saying "Looking forward to seeing you Thursday at 9:30, see you at The Forge" breaks through. Automate the repetitive stuff; keep the personal touch where it counts.

• **Measure ruthlessly**: Track open rates, click rates, booking conversion, and attendance. Test a new email subject line next week. You're not done after 6 weeks—you're building iteration into the system.

What's your current show-up rate on first classes for trial members?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Executing Role-Based Action Checklists for The Forge

**1. Start with outcome-obsessed KPIs, not task lists**
Don't build checklists around activities—build them around the ONE metric each person owns. Ask: "What does success look like for JoJo/Eilis/Sarah weekly?" (e.g., JoJo owns member retention rate, Eilis owns class utilization %, Sarah owns new member onboarding completion). Their checklist should be reverse-engineered from that number—what 3-5 actions *must* happen weekly to move it?

**2. Make them role-specific, not interchangeable**
Right now, there's overlap risk. Lock in: Which person owns sales pipeline? Member experience? Ops/scheduling? Staff performance? Once each woman has a *distinct* domain, their checklist becomes their sphere of control—no ambiguity, no "someone else will handle it."

**3. Build in a 10-minute Friday handoff ritual**
Each person submits their weekly KPI status (red/amber/green) + top blocker + one win by 4pm Friday. Grace reviews, identifies cross-functional dependencies, and flags priorities for Monday. This takes 20 minutes total and replaces vague "check-ins."

**4. Document it in a shared spreadsheet or Asana, not a PDF**
Checklists die in documents. Use a live tool where each role has her own tab—checklist items as tasks with due dates, auto-status visibility to Grace. One source of truth, weekly proof of execution.

**5. Run a 30-minute calibration meeting with all three**
Sit them down together,

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

• **Pre-session homework (send 48hrs before)**: Email the SSO + 90-day plan as a one-pager to each team member. Ask them to come with 3 questions ready. This kills the "deer in headlights" moment and lets them digest before you're all in a room.

• **Structure it as a "lane clarity" workshop, not a lecture**: Spend 60% of time on *their role in the 90 days*—literally write each person's 3-5 key priorities on a whiteboard with their name. Get verbal buy-in on each. People own what they helped define.

• **The SSO pitch takes 10 minutes max**: Vision fatigue is real. Hit the *why it matters to them personally*—e.g., "This plan gets us to hire 2 more instructors by Q2, which means your class load stabilizes." Connect SSO to their workload/job security.

• **End with a "blockers conversation"**: Ask "What do you need from me to crush your lane?" Write these down and commit to solving 2 before next week. Vague vision + real obstacles = nothing changes.

• **Document it**: Take a photo of the whiteboard with everyone's lanes. Drop it in Slack after. Reference it weekly in team touchbases—it keeps the session alive instead of becoming a forgotten PowerPoint.

What's your team size—is this 5 people or 15?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit for The Forge — 30-Day Execution

• **Map reality, not org charts** — Have Grace (or a trusted manager) shadow each team member for 2-3 shifts this week. Log actual time spent on: classes, member admin, cleaning, sales conversations, troubleshooting. Spreadsheet it. This beats guessing and catches hidden bottlenecks (e.g., one instructor always fixing the sound system).

• **Identify the bottleneck person first** — Don't rebalance equally. Find who's doing work that *only* they can do (e.g., a PT who's also handling all member onboarding). That's your immediate priority. Can classes be self-led? Can onboarding be templated and delegated to someone with 5 spare hours/week?

• **Create a "transfer list"** this weekend — 3-5 specific tasks moving FROM the over-stretched person TO someone with capacity. Be specific: "Sarah stops doing email scheduling (2hrs/week), Emma takes over." Agree dates. Get buy-in verbally before announcing.

• **Plug gaps with small shifts, not new hires** — Before hiring, ask: Can opening hours shift? Can a part-timer do 4 extra hours? Can members self-serve (e.g., app-based class bookings vs. staff-managed)? Test this first—it's faster and cheaper.

• **Quick win metric** — By day 30, the over-stretched person should report 1-2 hours/week freed up, or one admin task fully handed off. That's your proof it's working. Then repeat with the next person.

What role does Grace currently do day

# Cost Reduction Strategy for The Forge — 10% Target

**Audit your TOP 3 expense categories first** (likely: staff, rent, utilities). Don't spread yourself thin across everything. Calculate 10% of each — you'll probably hit your target by fixing 1-2 of these, not nibbling everywhere.

**Renegotiate supplier contracts TODAY — use competitive quotes as leverage.** Cleaning, equipment maintenance, towel services — most vendors expect this annually. Get 3 quotes, email your current suppliers saying "I have offers at X, happy to stay if you match." Most will.

**Cut gym floor redundancy, not value.** If you're running 6am-10pm with staff overlap on slow hours, shift to 1-2 morning coaches covering multiple stations. Keep peak hours (6-9am, 5-7pm) fully staffed — members notice this, not quiet afternoon gaps.

**Hack utilities ruthlessly.** LED lighting on sensors, water-efficient showers, zone heating (heat only occupied areas). Cost: ~£2-3k upfront, payback in 6-9 months on a gym's utilities bill.

**Don't cut anything members directly experience.** No reducing class frequency, equipment quality, or cleanliness. Growth stalls when members feel the squeeze. Cuts should be invisible operational efficiency — not member-facing.

**What's your biggest single expense right now?** That's where your 10% actually lives.

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — The Forge Edition

• **Set a hard deadline TODAY** — Pick the 3rd of next month (right after month-end), block 90 minutes, and do it then. Don't let this drift. Grace needs cash-flow visibility to spot problems (membership churn, low class uptake) before they compound.

• **Track these 5 metrics only** — Monthly Revenue (memberships + class packages), Member Acquisition Cost, Churn Rate, Average Revenue Per Member, and Cash Runway. Anything beyond this is distraction. Put them in a single Google Sheet, color-coded (red/green).

• **Pull raw data on the 1st** — Don't wait until day 3. Have bank statements, Stripe/payment processor exports, and gym management software (ClassPass, Zen, etc.) dumped into a folder immediately so you're not hunting for numbers under pressure.

• **Compare month-on-month, not just vs. budget** — Grace's budget might be wrong. What matters is: *Am I growing or shrinking compared to last month?* If churn spiked, that's the actionable insight; chase that down, not variance explanations.

• **Spend 15 minutes on ONE follow-up question** — After updating, ask: "What changed this month that moved the needle on the biggest metric?" (e.g., Did a new instructor bring members in? Did summer holidays tank attendance?). One insight beats perfect data.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull 12 months of actual data THIS WEEK** — membership revenue, class attendance patterns, cancellations by season, and any ancillary income (PT, merchandise). You can't forecast blind. Use spreadsheet or simple accounting software (Wave is free). This is your baseline.

• **Build a 3-scenario model (Conservative/Base/Optimistic)** — not one forecast. Conservative = retain current members + 5% churn. Base = your realistic target (e.g., add 15 members/month). Optimistic = marketing campaigns hit. Run each through payroll, rent, utilities. This shows Grace what levers actually move the needle.

• **Map membership cohorts by acquisition channel** — Facebook ads vs. word-of-mouth vs. walk-ins. Which members stick? Which churn fastest? This tells you which growth tactics to fund and which to kill. Forecast revenue differently for each source.

• **Lock in your "breakeven + X%" number** — what's the minimum monthly membership count and revenue needed to cover fixed costs PLUS reinvestment for growth? Anything above that is oxygen for scaling. Make this your north star metric.

• **Set a monthly review cadence (first Friday of month)** — compare actual vs. forecast in 15 minutes. Adjust forecast quarterly. Grace needs this rhythm to spot problems (churn spiking?) before they become crises.

**Start with month 1 actuals and one forward quarter. Don't perfectionism-delay this.**

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Tactical Execution

**Set up a quick spreadsheet today with these columns:**
- Service line (each class type, PT packages, add-ons like smoothies/merchandise)
- Monthly revenue (actual figures from last 3 months)
- Direct costs ONLY: instructor pay, equipment wear, ingredients if applicable
- Contribution margin % (revenue minus direct costs, divided by revenue)
- *Don't* allocate rent/utilities yet — you need to see raw profitability first

**Segment PT differently than group classes:**
- PT likely has higher margins but lower volume — calculate per-session-type (1-on-1 vs small group), not bundled packages
- Note which PT packages have early cancellations (eats margin fast)

**For add-ons, track unit economics ruthlessly:**
- Smoothie bar: actual cost per drink, waste %, selling price = real margin
- Merchandise: cost, sell price, inventory turnover (slow movers kill profit)
- Extras like locker rentals: negligible cost, but high margin — these might punch above their weight

**Action this week:**
- Pull last 3 months' transaction data + instructor invoices (fastest way = ask your gym software provider for a revenue report, they usually have this pre-built)
- Identify your top 3 margin winners and bottom 2 performers immediately
- Grace, this tells you whether to double down on high-margin services or kill unprofitable ones — the insight is worth 2 hours of work

What's your biggest cost category right now — payroll or rent?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan for The Forge Automation Audit

• **Map it visually first (30 mins)**: Screenshot every active automation in your system (Zapier/Make/native platform). Paste into a Google Sheet with columns: Trigger | Action | Where it sends data | Last tested date. This reveals duplicates instantly — you'll spot multiple automations sending enquiries to the same place.

• **Test the critical path live**: Have someone actually fill out your "Join Us" form TODAY. Track where that enquiry lands: does it hit your email? Your CRM? Does it auto-trigger a trial-booking link? Identify exactly where it stalls. Most gyms have enquiries disappearing into a black hole between form submission and your trial-booking confirmation.

• **Kill the low-value duplicates ruthlessly**: If you've got three automations notifying you of new enquiries (email + Slack + CRM), you only need ONE that feeds everything into your CRM. The rest are noise creating confusion about who's contacted you.

• **Close the trial-booking gap**: This is where money leaks. Ensure new enquiries automatically get a personalised message within 2 hours with a direct link to book their trial slot (not "email us back"). Use your calendar system (Calendly/Acuity) to auto-schedule, not manual back-and-forth.

• **Document and assign**: Once it's working, write down the exact sequence in a one-page doc. Assign someone (admin/manager) to test it quarterly — automations break silently.

**Priority**: Get enquiry-to-trial-booking to 100% automated and sub-2-hour response. That's your real growth lever.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation: 3 Tasks to Hit First at The Forge

**1. Membership Inquiry Response Loop**
- Set up a ChatGPT-powered chatbot (use Tidio or ManyChat) connected to your Instagram/Facebook that answers: pricing tiers, class schedule, trial session booking, "what's included" questions
- **Action today:** Paste your FAQ + pricing into a simple prompt template, test 5 real inquiries to refine responses, go live this week
- **Impact:** Converts 2-3x more inbound leads without Grace or staff touching them; frees up 5+ hours weekly

**2. Class Attendance Reminders + No-Show Prevention**
- Automate SMS/email 24 hours before booked classes with a "confirm or reschedule" link (use Zendesk or Zapier + Stripe API if you're already collecting bookings digitally)
- **Action today:** Audit your current booking system—if manual, switch to a free tier of Acuity Scheduling or Mindbody; set up one reminder template this week
- **Impact:** Cuts no-shows by 30-40%; improves predictable class capacity planning

**3. Member Progress/Retention Check-ins**
- Build a simple Zapier automation: members inactive 2+ weeks get an auto-personalized email ("Hi [Name], we miss you—here's 20% off your next 3 classes") pulling data from your membership database
- **Action today:** Map out who's gone cold in the last month; write 2-3 message variants; set Zapier rule to run weekly
- **Impact:** Reactivates dormant

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Don't build from scratch — use WordPress security plugins TODAY** (Wordfence free tier takes 30 mins to install). This covers 80% of attacks without hiring anyone. If you're on Shopify/Wix, those platforms handle most backend security automatically — focus only on strong password protocols instead.

• **Get an SSL certificate installed immediately** (your hosting provider likely offers free Let's Encrypt). This takes one support ticket. Without it, you're losing customer trust AND Google ranking — women researching memberships see "not secure" warnings and bounce.

• **Audit who has admin access RIGHT NOW** — Grace, your web person, maybe 1 other. Delete any old contractor logins. This alone prevents 60% of gym industry breaches (usually accidental access misuse, not hackers).

• **Set up basic email/payment security this week:** If you're collecting payment info, use Stripe or PayPal (never store cards yourself — legal nightmare). For member emails/data, enable two-factor authentication on your hosting account and any CRM.

• **Schedule one 30-min consultation with a local IT firm** (€100-150) to do a quick vulnerability scan and give you a prioritized list. This gives you peace of mind and a roadmap that's specific to YOUR setup — way cheaper than reactive breaches.

**Priority order:** SSL → plugin setup → access audit → payment security. All doable this week without major costs.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has ballooned 350% (£6 → £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 → £109). This isn't a scaling problem—it's a creative or audience decay problem masquerading as budget constraint. Your close rate held strong at 75% in July, so the offer and nurture are sound; the issue is lead *quality* and *volume* have both cratered. The June dip (£20/lead, 21 leads) should have been your warning flag that creative was fatiguing or audience was thinning.

You've likely hit audience saturation or creative decay on a tight geographic footprint (Belfast is small). Spend dropped dramatically but CPL didn't recover—meaning either you cut budget mid-cycle (losing scale economies), or algorithm couldn't find fresh, cold prospects at any price. Either way, July's 4 leads off £109 spend is unsustainable unless you're testing.

**Actions**

1. **Audit creative immediately.** Pull the July ads and compare thumbnails/copy to April winners. Are you running the same hero creative from April (which pulled £6/lead)? If yes, pause it now—it's exhausted. Test 3 fresh video/image angles: (a) member transformation/body confidence angle, (b) community/friendship angle (powerful for women-only gyms), (c) "no ego" or intimidation-free angle. Launch each with fresh ad copy at £40 budget to find the new winner.

2. **Expand or refresh audience targeting.** Your Belfast audience pool is finite. Test: (a) Lookalike audiences built from your Jul/Jun converters (even though volume is low, they're proven), (b) Detailed targeting swap—if you're currently broad + "gym interests," tighten to "fitness," "health," "wellness" OR loosen to catch "yoga," "pilates," "self-improvement" (women-only gyms appeal beyond hardcore gym-goers), (c) Add a saved audience: women 25-45, Belfast + 2-mile radius, college-educated (proxy for disposable income for a premium trial).

3. **Reset budget to £300/month and
